content.text: Impact Mitigation Tracking

Identifying algorithmic impacts without a systematic process for resolving them produces only documentation debt. Impact Mitigation Tracking (IMT) is the discipline of converting an Algorithmic Impact Assessment's findings into concrete actions, assigning owners, measuring progress, and verifying that residual risk has been reduced to an acceptable level before or after agent deployment.
Why It Matters

Regulators and auditors do not accept static reports. The EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, and ISO 42001 all require evidence that identified risks are actively managed and that mitigation effectiveness is measured over time. Without a tracking system, organizations face:
Mitigation items falling through the cracks because ownership is unclear
No evidence trail for compliance reviews that mitigations were actually implemented
Residual risks accepted implicitly rather than reviewed by the appropriate authority
Recurrence of the same algorithmic harms across successive agent versions
Audit findings elevated to critical because a known risk was left unaddressed
A well-run IMT program converts the AIA from a one-time gate into a living control that actively reduces algorithmic harm.
Core Concepts and Benefits
Impact Registry

The impact registry is the single authoritative ledger of all identified algorithmic impacts. Each entry captures: the impact identifier, description, affected population, severity, likelihood, the mitigation strategy chosen, owner, due date, status, evidence of completion, and residual risk level after mitigation. Nothing lives in email threads or slide decks — the registry is the record of authority.
Mitigation Strategies
Mitigations fall into five canonical types:
Avoid: eliminate the agent feature or decision that causes the impact (strongest, but may reduce functionality)
Reduce: lower the probability or magnitude — retrain on balanced data, tighten guardrails, add human review
Transfer: shift responsibility to a third party who is better placed to control the risk (vendor contract, insurance)
Accept: formally document that the residual risk is within tolerance, with named approver at the appropriate authority level
Monitor: deploy continuous detection (dashboards, alerts) so that impact can be caught quickly if it materialises
Benefits
Turns AIA findings into auditable evidence of active risk management
Drives clear ownership — every impact has exactly one named owner accountable for closure
Enables escalation before problems become incidents or regulatory findings
Provides a feedback loop: completed mitigations are retested to confirm effectiveness before closure
Supports continuous improvement by revealing patterns (e.g. data quality is the root cause of 60% of impacts)
Trade-offs and Limitations
Registry overhead: maintaining the registry requires discipline and tooling; under-resourced teams let it go stale
False closure: mitigations are marked done without verifying effectiveness — residual risk stays high but appears closed
Scope creep: every subsequent monitoring alert reopens old items, creating an ever-growing backlog that demoralises teams
Quantification limits: for societal harms (e.g. disparate impact on minority groups), residual risk scores are estimates, not measurements
Velocity tension: thorough mitigation cycles extend deployment timelines; business pressure can lead to premature acceptance
How It Works
The IMT process runs in five stages, illustrated in the diagram below:

attrs.alt: Impact Mitigation Tracking Lifecycle — from registry through residual risk review

content.text: Stage 1: Capture to Registry

Each impact identified in the AIA is assigned a unique identifier (e.g. IMP-2024-047) and logged in the registry with its description, affected scope, severity (Critical/High/Medium/Low), and likelihood. The registry is the source of truth from this point forward.
Stage 2: Risk Assessment

The inherent risk score is calculated as severity × likelihood on a 5×5 matrix, producing a risk level (1-5: Very Low through Critical). This score determines the mitigation urgency and the authority level required to accept residual risk: Critical risks require executive sign-off; High risks require senior management; Medium and Low risks can be accepted by the AI product owner.
Stage 3: Mitigation Planning

For each impact, the owner selects a mitigation strategy, defines measurable success criteria, identifies required resources, and sets a due date. Due dates are risk-tiered: Critical = 30 days, High = 60 days, Medium = 90 days, Low = next quarterly review. The plan is reviewed by the AI Risk Committee before implementation begins.
Stage 4: Implementation and Tracking

Status transitions: Open → In Progress → Pending Verification → Closed (or Accepted). Evidence is attached at each transition — a PR link, test results, audit log excerpt, or formal sign-off. Overdue items trigger automated alerts to the owner and their manager at day 0 (due date), day 7, and day 30.
Stage 5: Residual Risk Review

After a mitigation is implemented, the risk is reassessed. If residual risk is within tolerance, the item is closed with evidence. If residual risk remains high, the item returns to Stage 3 for an additional mitigation. If residual risk is accepted as unavoidable, a formal acceptance record is signed at the required authority level and attached to the registry entry.
Architecture and Data Model

The impact registry can be maintained in a spreadsheet for small programs, a Jira/Linear project for engineering-integrated teams, or a dedicated GRC platform (ServiceNow, Archer, OneTrust) for enterprise programs. The schema below is the minimum viable structure regardless of tooling:

# Minimum viable impact registry schema (Pydantic)
from enum import Enum
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class Severity(str, Enum):
    critical = "Critical"
    high     = "High"
    medium   = "Medium"
    low      = "Low"

class MitigationStrategy(str, Enum):
    avoid    = "Avoid"
    reduce   = "Reduce"
    transfer = "Transfer"
    accept   = "Accept"
    monitor  = "Monitor"

class Status(str, Enum):
    open               = "Open"
    in_progress        = "In Progress"
    pending_verification = "Pending Verification"
    closed             = "Closed"
    accepted           = "Accepted"

class ImpactEntry(BaseModel):
    impact_id: str                     # e.g. "IMP-2024-047"
    agent_id: str                      # e.g. "agent-loan-underwriting-v2"
    description: str
    affected_population: str
    severity: Severity
    likelihood: int = Field(ge=1, le=5)
    inherent_risk_score: int           # severity_weight * likelihood
    mitigation_strategy: MitigationStrategy
    mitigation_description: str
    owner: str                         # email of accountable person
    due_date: date
    status: Status
    evidence_links: list[str] = []     # PR URLs, test report paths, etc.
    residual_risk_score: Optional[int] = None
    residual_risk_accepted_by: Optional[str] = None
    residual_acceptance_date: Optional[date] = None
    notes: str = ""

# Inherent risk score weights
SEVERITY_WEIGHT = {
    Severity.critical: 5,
    Severity.high: 4,
    Severity.medium: 3,
    Severity.low: 2,
}

def inherent_risk(entry: ImpactEntry) -> int:
    return SEVERITY_WEIGHT[entry.severity] * entry.likelihood
When to Use Impact Mitigation Tracking
Any AI agent subject to an Algorithmic Impact Assessment — IMT is the mandatory follow-through
Post-incident reviews where a harm has already occurred and mitigations must be verified before redeployment
Regulatory obligations such as EU AI Act Article 9 (risk management system) and NIST AI RMF Govern/Manage functions
Merger and acquisition due diligence — acquirers want evidence that known AI risks are being actively managed
ISO 42001 certification — auditors require evidence of an operational risk treatment process, not just a risk register
Implementation Guide
Step 1: Seed the Registry from the AIA

Automatically convert each AIA finding into a registry entry. Do not let humans manually transcribe — every impact finding should produce exactly one registry row, preserving the AIA's finding identifier as a foreign key.

import json
from datetime import date, timedelta
from impact_registry import ImpactEntry, Severity, MitigationStrategy, Status, inherent_risk

DUE_DATE_DAYS = {
    Severity.critical: 30,
    Severity.high: 60,
    Severity.medium: 90,
    Severity.low: 180,
}

def seed_registry_from_aia(aia_path: str) -> list[ImpactEntry]:
    """Convert AIA JSON findings into registry entries."""
    with open(aia_path) as f:
        aia = json.load(f)

    entries = []
    for finding in aia["findings"]:
        sev = Severity(finding["severity"])
        likelihood = finding["likelihood"]  # 1-5 integer
        entry = ImpactEntry(
            impact_id=f"IMP-{finding['aia_id']}-{finding['seq']:03d}",
            agent_id=aia["agent_id"],
            description=finding["description"],
            affected_population=finding["affected_population"],
            severity=sev,
            likelihood=likelihood,
            inherent_risk_score=SEVERITY_WEIGHT[sev] * likelihood,
            mitigation_strategy=MitigationStrategy.reduce,  # default; owner refines
            mitigation_description="TBD — owner to define within 5 business days",
            owner=finding.get("proposed_owner", "ai-risk-team@company.com"),
            due_date=date.today() + timedelta(days=DUE_DATE_DAYS[sev]),
            status=Status.open,
        )
        entries.append(entry)
    return entries

# Example usage
entries = seed_registry_from_aia("aia-loan-agent-v2.json")
print(f"Seeded {len(entries)} registry entries")
for e in entries:
    print(f"  {e.impact_id}: {e.severity.value} — due {e.due_date}")
Step 2: Assign Owners and Refine Plans

Within five business days of seeding, each owner must confirm their assignment, refine the mitigation description, and specify measurable success criteria. Unconfirmed assignments after five days escalate to the AI Risk Program Manager.
Step 3: Automated Status Tracking
Step 4: Verification Before Closure

Before marking an item Closed, the owner must provide evidence that the mitigation is in place and effective. For bias mitigations, this means a fairness evaluation report comparing demographic parity metrics before and after. For data quality mitigations, a data quality scorecard. For access control mitigations, a penetration test or access review report. The AI Risk Committee reviews all Critical and High closures.
Step 5: Residual Risk Acceptance

def accept_residual_risk(
    entry: ImpactEntry,
    residual_score: int,
    accepted_by: str,
    rationale: str,
) -> ImpactEntry:
    """
    Formally accept residual risk after mitigation.
    Critical/High items require executive approver.
    """
    if entry.severity in (Severity.critical, Severity.high):
        # Validate approver has executive authority (check HR system in production)
        if not is_executive(accepted_by):
            raise PermissionError(
                f"{accepted_by} lacks authority to accept {entry.severity.value} residual risk"
            )
    entry.residual_risk_score = residual_score
    entry.residual_risk_accepted_by = accepted_by
    entry.residual_acceptance_date = date.today()
    entry.status = Status.accepted
    entry.notes += f"\nResidual acceptance: {rationale}"
    return entry
Real-World Example: Credit Decision Agent
A financial institution deployed a credit decision agent (CDA-v3) that scored loan applicants. The AIA identified three impacts:
IMP-CDA-001 (High): Disparate impact — approval rate for Hispanic applicants was 12 percentage points lower than for similarly-qualified White applicants, violating ECOA
IMP-CDA-002 (Medium): Data completeness — 18% of applicants had incomplete employment history, causing the model to default to low credit scores
IMP-CDA-003 (Low): Explainability gap — loan officers could not explain denials in plain language as required by the Fair Credit Reporting Act
Mitigation tracking outcomes after 90 days:
Impact
Strategy
Action Taken
Residual Risk
Status
IMP-CDA-001
Reduce
Retrained on fair-lending-balanced dataset; added demographic parity constraint
Low (1.8pp gap)
Closed — Executive approved
IMP-CDA-002
Reduce
Added data enrichment step using bureau supplemental data
Low (4% missing)
Closed — Risk Owner approved
IMP-CDA-003
Reduce
Integrated LIME explanation module; built officer training
Very Low
Closed — Risk Owner approved
The registry provided the evidence trail for the institution's CRA (Community Reinvestment Act) exam, and regulators noted the active mitigation process as a positive control.
Common Mistakes
Marking items closed without evidence: the registry shows Closed but no evidence link is attached — fails any audit
Owner is a team, not a person: team ownership means no-one is accountable; every item must have exactly one named individual
Setting all due dates to the same far-future date to avoid overdue alerts — regulatory-tiered due dates exist for a reason
Forgetting to re-assess residual risk after mitigation — treating mitigation completion as equivalent to risk closure
Not linking back to the AIA: if you cannot show which AIA finding a registry entry addresses, auditors cannot follow the chain of evidence
Accepting Critical risk at manager level when executive sign-off is required — creates a governance deficiency finding
Never closing items by marking them Accepted — some mitigations cannot reduce risk to zero; formal acceptance is a valid outcome when done at the right authority level
Best Practices
Use a stable impact identifier scheme (IMP-{YEAR}-{SEQ}) so items can be referenced in audit reports and incident tickets
Automate overdue detection and route alerts through the same channels the team already uses (Slack, Jira, PagerDuty)
Set a quarterly registry review cadence even for items that are not overdue — circumstances change and a previously-accepted Low risk may escalate
Track effectiveness separately from implementation: an item is Closed only when evidence shows the residual risk is lower, not merely when the mitigation code is deployed
Publish a monthly dashboard to the AI Governance Committee showing: total open items by severity, average time-to-close by tier, and percentage overdue
For high-severity items, require a sign-off meeting with at minimum the owner, the AI Risk lead, and a legal representative before closure
Version-lock the registry snapshot at each agent release — so you can show exactly which impacts were open vs. closed at the time of each deployment
Performance and Scalability
A large AI program may track hundreds of impact entries across dozens of agents. At this scale, manual spreadsheet management fails. Key scalability patterns:
Database-backed registry: store entries in a relational database (PostgreSQL), query by severity, status, and agent_id for reporting
Automated seeding: CI/CD pipeline runs the AIA and pipes findings directly into the registry — no manual entry
GRC platform integration: enterprise programs integrate with ServiceNow Risk Management or Archer to leverage existing workflow automation and reporting
Aggregated dashboards: roll up metrics across all agents into a single executive view — total open items, average age, closure rate by quarter
API-first design: expose the registry as a REST API so the AIA tool, incident management system, and compliance reporting tool can all read and write without duplicating data

Comparison: Ad-Hoc vs. Structured IMT
Dimension
Ad-Hoc Tracking
Structured IMT
Ownership
Implied or shared across team
Exactly one named individual per impact
Due dates
Best-effort, often missed
Risk-tiered, automated escalation
Closure evidence
Verbal confirmation
Linked evidence artifacts required
Residual risk review
Rarely performed
Mandatory before closure or acceptance
Audit readiness

Registry is the audit artifact

Residual risk score compared pre/post
Regulatory compliance
Fails EU AI Act / NIST AI RMF requirements
Satisfies Article 9 (risk management system)
Security and Reliability of the Tracking System Itself
Access control: only the impact owner and AI Risk lead should be able to close or accept items — prevent self-certification of high-severity risks
Immutable audit log: every status change and evidence update is recorded with timestamp and user identity; the log must be append-only
Backup and retention: the registry must be retained for at least as long as the agent is in production plus any regulatory retention period (often 7 years in financial services)
Integrity verification: use cryptographic signing or hash chaining on closure records so tampering is detectable during audits
Separation of duties: the person implementing a mitigation must not be the sole approver of closure for High/Critical items
Key Takeaways
Impact Mitigation Tracking is the operational follow-through to the Algorithmic Impact Assessment — without it, the AIA produces documentation rather than risk reduction
Every identified impact must have a unique ID, a named owner, a due date, and evidence of closure in the registry
Mitigation strategies are: Avoid, Reduce, Transfer, Accept, Monitor — choose the strongest feasible option for the severity level
Closure requires evidence that residual risk is lower — not just that the mitigation was implemented
Residual risk acceptance must be signed at the authority level matching the impact severity: Critical = executive, High = senior management
Automate overdue detection and publish monthly dashboards to the AI Governance Committee
At audit time, the impact registry is the evidence that demonstrates an operational AI risk management system — not the AIA document itself
